narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as a tougher climb—landing at a 64% chance of not passing. Picture it like this: you're starting from a challenging spot academically, with your Year 1 grades placing you in the bottom decile of your school, and even by Year 3, you're still in that lowest decile, which stood out as the biggest signal to the model that sustaining the high performance needed for the bar might be an uphill battle. Your LSAT score of 32 reinforced that concern, as it suggested the kind of analytical firepower typically required for bar success wasn't quite there yet. Layered on top, your undergraduate GPA of 3.1 painted a picture of steady but not standout academic momentum coming into law school. Your lower-middle family income added another layer, hinting at potential stresses or fewer resources that could make intense bar prep harder to manage. Elements like your Native American background, being male, and planning full-time enrollment all nudged the prediction toward higher risk in the model's view of patterns from past students. It's not that any one thing doomed your chances—it's this combination that together sketched a profile where others with stronger starts in grades and test scores tended to falter on the bar. You've got real determination, though, and focusing on boosting those early academic deciles or LSAT-like skills could shift this story next time. Hang in there; this is just one chapter.
